reasoning: The 360-day average of 20.5 protests per 30 days implies a high mean, likely inflated by significant protest waves earlier in the year (such as global demonstrations over the Gaza conflict). The distribution of event data like protests is typically right-skewed, meaning the median is lower than the mean. Consequently, a randomly selected 30-day window is more likely to fall below this average unless a major protest wave occurs. A comprehensive review of Cypriot news in February and early March 2026 reveals a few discrete protests within the February 6 - March 7 window — such as a farmers’ tractor protest in Nicosia on February 16, an Iranian solidarity gathering on February 14, and some activity related to Gaza flotilla protests on February 9. However, we did not find evidence of sustained, widespread, daily protest action throughout the window. High-frequency events from late January/early February (like the gig workers' nationwide strike on Feb 3) fall outside the target timeframe. While Cyprus holds the EU Council Presidency, driving some baseline activity, the observed schedule seems typical and unlikely to individually produce the >20 discrete ACLED events needed to clear the 20.5 mean. Therefore, the probability leans lower than 50%.
